questions.question: Bryophytes are commonly called:

options.B: Amphibians of the plant kingdom

questions.explanation: Bryophytes are called 'amphibians of the plant kingdom' because they can live in soil but are dependent on water for sexual reproduction.

questions.question: Why are bryophytes called amphibians of the plant kingdom?

options.B: They live only on land
options.C: They can live in soil but are dependent on water for sexual reproduction
options.D: They have both aquatic and terrestrial reproductive structures

questions.explanation: Bryophytes are called amphibians of the plant kingdom because they can live in soil but are dependent on water for sexual reproduction (like amphibians in animals).

questions.question: Bryophytes are found in which habitats?

options.B: Moist, shaded areas — rocks, soil, tree bark in moist, humid areas

questions.explanation: Bryophytes are found in moist, shaded areas in the hills. They grow on rocks, soil and bark of trees in moist and humid areas.

questions.question: What type of plant body do bryophytes have?

options.A: Differentiated into true roots, stems and leaves
options.B: More differentiated than algae — thallus-like, prostrate or erect, with root-like, leaf-like and stem-like structures

questions.explanation: The plant body of bryophytes is more differentiated than that of algae — it is thallus-like, prostrate or erect, and attached to the substratum by root-like, multicellular and branched rhizoids. They have leaf-like and stem-like structures but lack true vascular tissue.

questions.question: What are rhizoids in bryophytes?

options.A: True roots with xylem and phloem
options.B: Root-like, unicellular or multicellular branched structures that attach the plant to substratum

questions.explanation: Rhizoids are root-like structures in bryophytes that attach the plant body to the substratum. They are unicellular in liverworts and multicellular and branched in mosses.

questions.question: Do bryophytes have true vascular tissue?

options.A: Yes, they have well-developed xylem and phloem
options.B: No, they do not have specialized vascular tissue
options.C: Yes, but only xylem, no phloem
options.D: Yes, but only phloem, no xylem

questions.explanation: Bryophytes do NOT have specialized vascular tissue (xylem and phloem). They lack true vascular tissue, which is why they are called 'non-vascular plants' or placed below pteridophytes in plant evolution.

questions.question: The main plant body of a bryophyte is:

options.B: Haploid gametophyte

questions.explanation: The main plant body of a bryophyte is the haploid gametophyte. The sporophyte is dependent on the gametophyte.

questions.question: The sex organs in bryophytes are:

options.B: Multicellular and jacketed

questions.explanation: The sex organs in bryophytes are multicellular and jacketed (surrounded by a layer of sterile cells).

questions.question: The male sex organ in bryophytes is called:

options.B: Antheridium

questions.explanation: The male sex organ in bryophytes is called antheridium (plural: antheridia). It produces biflagellate antherozoids (male gametes).

questions.question: The female sex organ in bryophytes is called:

options.B: Oogonium

questions.explanation: The female sex organ in bryophytes is called archegonium (plural: archegonia). It is flask-shaped and produces the egg cell.

questions.question: The male gametes produced by antheridia in bryophytes are:

options.B: Biflagellate antherozoids

questions.explanation: The antheridia in bryophytes produce biflagellate antherozoids (male gametes) that are motile and require water for fertilisation.

questions.question: Why is water essential for sexual reproduction in bryophytes?

options.B: The biflagellate antherozoids need water as a medium to swim to the archegonium for fertilisation
options.D: Water is needed for spore germination only

questions.explanation: Water is essential for sexual reproduction in bryophytes because the biflagellate antherozoids (male gametes) are motile and need water as a medium to swim to the archegonium where the egg is present.

questions.question: Fertilisation in bryophytes results in the formation of:

options.B: A zygote which develops into a sporophyte

questions.explanation: Fertilisation (fusion of antherozoid with egg) in bryophytes results in the formation of a zygote which develops into a sporophyte.

questions.question: The sporophyte in bryophytes is:

options.B: Partially or wholly dependent on the photosynthetic gametophyte

questions.explanation: The sporophyte in bryophytes is not free-living — it is partially or wholly dependent on the photosynthetic gametophyte for nutrition.

questions.question: The sporophyte of bryophytes is differentiated into:

options.B: Foot, seta and capsule

questions.explanation: The sporophyte of bryophytes is differentiated into foot (embedded in gametophyte), seta (stalk) and capsule (which produces spores by meiosis).

questions.question: In the bryophyte sporophyte, the foot is:

options.B: The stalk
options.C: The embedded part that absorbs nutrients from gametophyte

questions.explanation: In the bryophyte sporophyte, the foot is the embedded part that absorbs nutrients from the gametophyte.

questions.question: The seta in bryophyte sporophyte is:

options.B: The foot embedded in gametophyte
options.C: The stalk connecting foot to capsule

questions.explanation: The seta in the bryophyte sporophyte is the stalk that connects the foot (embedded in gametophyte) to the capsule (spore-producing structure).

questions.question: Spores in the capsule of bryophyte sporophyte are produced by:

options.B: Meiosis

questions.explanation: The capsule of the bryophyte sporophyte produces spores by meiosis (meiotic division), so the spores are haploid.

questions.question: The spores produced in the bryophyte capsule germinate to produce:

options.B: A protonema and then free-living gametophyte

questions.explanation: Spores produced in the capsule by meiosis germinate to produce a protonema (free-living, green, filamentous, branched structure) which then develops into a free-living gametophyte.

questions.question: The life cycle of bryophytes shows alternation of generations. The two phases are:

options.B: Haploid gametophyte (dominant) and diploid sporophyte (dependent)

questions.explanation: Bryophytes show alternation of generations with a dominant haploid gametophyte and a diploid sporophyte that is dependent on the gametophyte.

questions.question: In bryophytes, which generation is dominant?

options.B: Gametophyte (haploid)

questions.explanation: In bryophytes, the gametophyte (haploid) is the dominant phase. The sporophyte (diploid) is dependent on the gametophyte.

questions.question: Bryophytes include which of the following groups?

options.B: Liverworts, hornworts and mosses

questions.explanation: Bryophytes include liverworts (Hepaticopsida), hornworts (Anthocerotopsida) and mosses (Bryopsida).

questions.question: Liverworts belong to which class of Bryophyta?

options.B: Anthocerotopsida

questions.explanation: Liverworts belong to class Hepaticopsida of Bryophyta. The name 'liverwort' refers to their liver-shaped thallus.

questions.question: Mosses belong to which class of Bryophyta?

options.B: Anthocerotopsida

questions.explanation: Mosses belong to class Bryopsida of Bryophyta.

questions.question: The habitat of liverworts is:

options.B: Moist, shaded and humid localities

questions.explanation: Liverworts are found in moist, shaded and humid localities — rocks, soil and bark of trees in moist and humid areas.

questions.question: The plant body of liverworts is:

options.B: Thalloid — dorsiventrally flattened, lobed and closely appressed to the substratum

questions.explanation: The plant body of a liverwort is thalloid — dorsiventrally flattened, lobed and closely appressed to the substrate.

questions.question: Which is a well-known example of a liverwort?

options.B: Polytrichum

questions.explanation: Marchantia is a well-known example of a liverwort (Hepaticopsida). Funaria, Polytrichum and Sphagnum are mosses.

questions.question: Vegetative reproduction in liverworts occurs by:

options.B: Fragmentation and through gemmae

questions.explanation: Vegetative reproduction in liverworts takes place by fragmentation of thalli and by formation of gemmae.

questions.question: Gemmae are:

options.B: Green, multicellular, asexual buds developed in small receptacles called gemma cups

questions.explanation: Gemmae are green, multicellular, asexual buds which develop in small receptacles called gemma cups located on the thalli. They are a means of vegetative reproduction.

questions.question: Gemma cups are found in:

options.B: On the thalli of liverworts like Marchantia

questions.explanation: Gemma cups are small receptacles located on the thalli of liverworts (like Marchantia). Gemmae develop within these cups.

questions.question: What happens to gemmae after they are detached from the parent plant?

options.B: They get dispersed and germinate to form new, genetically identical individuals

questions.explanation: Gemmae get detached from the parent plant and germinate to form new and genetically identical individuals — they are a means of asexual vegetative reproduction.

questions.question: Sexual reproduction in liverworts involves formation of:

options.B: Only archegonia

questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction in liverworts involves the formation of antheridia (male sex organs) and archegonia (female sex organs).

questions.question: After fertilisation in liverworts, the zygote develops into:

options.B: A sporophyte differentiated into foot, seta and capsule

questions.explanation: After fertilisation, the zygote develops into a sporophyte which is differentiated into foot (embedded in gametophyte), seta and capsule.

questions.question: Spores from the liverwort capsule germinate to produce:

options.B: A free-living gametophyte

questions.explanation: Spores from the liverwort capsule germinate to produce free-living gametophytes (thalli).

questions.question: Marchantia is an example of a liverwort that shows:

options.B: Both vegetative (fragmentation and gemmae) and sexual reproduction

questions.explanation: Marchantia shows both vegetative reproduction (fragmentation and gemmae) and sexual reproduction (through antheridia and archegonia).

questions.question: Riccia is a common example of which group?

options.B: Liverwort

questions.explanation: Riccia is a common example of a liverwort (Hepaticopsida) with a simple thalloid plant body.

questions.question: Pellia is classified as a:

options.B: Liverwort

questions.explanation: Pellia is a liverwort (Hepaticopsida) with a simple thalloid body.

questions.question: Porella is classified as a:

options.B: Liverwort

questions.explanation: Porella is a liverwort (Hepaticopsida). It is a leafy liverwort with more differentiated structure.

questions.question: In liverworts, are the sex organs produced on the same plant or different plants?

options.B: May be on the same plant or different plants depending on the species

questions.explanation: In liverworts, the sex organs may be on the same plant (monoecious) or different plants (dioecious) depending on the species. Marchantia is dioecious.

questions.question: The dominant free-living phase in liverworts is:

options.B: Haploid gametophyte (the thallus)

questions.explanation: The dominant free-living phase in liverworts is the haploid gametophyte (the thalloid plant body we see). The sporophyte is dependent on it.

questions.question: Which of the following structures is unique to liverworts as a vegetative reproductive structure?

options.B: Gemmae in gemma cups

questions.explanation: Gemmae in gemma cups are structures unique to liverworts for vegetative reproduction. They are green, multicellular, asexual buds that detach and form new individuals.

questions.question: Liverworts are considered more advanced than algae because:

options.B: They have a more complex plant body and multicellular jacketed sex organs

questions.explanation: Liverworts are more advanced than algae because they have a more differentiated plant body, multicellular jacketed sex organs (antheridia and archegonia), and show an alternation of generations.

questions.question: The rhizoids of liverworts are:

options.B: Unicellular

questions.explanation: The rhizoids of liverworts are unicellular, unlike the multicellular branched rhizoids of mosses.

questions.question: In which part of the liverwort plant body are the rhizoids found?

options.B: On the ventral surface of the thallus
options.C: At the tips of the thallus

questions.explanation: Rhizoids are found on the ventral (lower) surface of the liverwort thallus. They anchor the thallus to the substrate.

questions.question: The thallus of Marchantia is described as:

options.B: Dorsiventrally flattened, lobed, closely appressed to substrate

questions.explanation: The thallus of Marchantia (and other liverworts) is dorsiventrally flattened, lobed and closely appressed to the substrate.

questions.question: What is 'dorsiventrally flattened' as described for liverwort thallus?

options.A: The thallus is shaped like a sphere
options.B: The thallus has distinct upper (dorsal) and lower (ventral) surfaces and is flat

questions.explanation: Dorsiventrally flattened means the thallus has a distinct upper (dorsal) surface and lower (ventral) surface and is flattened — like a ribbon lying flat on the substrate.

questions.question: In the life cycle of liverworts, what is the correct sequence?

options.A: Spore → Gametophyte → Gametes → Zygote → Sporophyte → Spore
options.B: Gametophyte → Spore → Sporophyte → Gametes → Zygote

questions.explanation: Correct sequence in liverwort life cycle: Haploid spore → germinate → free-living haploid gametophyte → produce gametes (antherozoids + egg) → fertilisation → zygote → diploid sporophyte (foot + seta + capsule) → meiosis in capsule → spores → cycle repeats.

questions.question: Which type of plant body organization do liverworts show?

options.A: Thalloid plant body (no differentiation into root, stem, leaf)
options.B: Body with true root, stem and leaves

questions.explanation: Liverworts have a thalloid plant body — it is not differentiated into true root, stem and leaves. They represent an intermediate stage between algae and higher plants.

questions.question: Mosses are more complex than liverworts because:

options.B: Mosses have a protonema stage and an upright leafy gametophyte with stem-like and leaf-like structures

questions.explanation: Mosses are more complex than liverworts because they have a protonema stage (filamentous) and an upright leafy gametophyte bearing stem-like axis and leaf-like appendages.

questions.question: The gametophyte of mosses has which stages?

options.B: Only leafy gametophore
options.C: Two stages: protonema (filamentous) and leafy gametophore

questions.explanation: The gametophyte of mosses has two stages: (1) first stage is protonema — a creeping, green, branched filamentous structure; (2) second stage is the leafy stage — an upright, slender, gametophore bearing stem-like axis with leaf-like appendages.

questions.question: What is a protonema in mosses?

options.B: A creeping, green, branched, filamentous structure that develops from spore germination

questions.explanation: The protonema is the first stage of the moss gametophyte. It is a creeping, green, branched, filamentous stage that develops directly from the germination of spores.

questions.question: From the protonema, what develops in mosses?

options.B: Leafy gametophore (the upright, leafy gametophyte)

questions.explanation: From the protonema, numerous buds are formed. These buds give rise to the leafy gametophore — the upright, slender gametophyte bearing stem-like axis with leaf-like appendages.

questions.question: The rhizoids of mosses are:

options.B: Multicellular and branched

questions.explanation: The rhizoids of mosses are multicellular and branched, unlike the unicellular rhizoids of liverworts.

questions.question: Examples of mosses mentioned in NCERT include:

options.B: Funaria, Polytrichum and Sphagnum

questions.explanation: Examples of mosses (Bryopsida) include Funaria (common moss), Polytrichum and Sphagnum (peat moss).

questions.question: Funaria is an example of a:

options.B: Moss

questions.explanation: Funaria (commonly called cord moss or water moss) is a common example of a moss (Bryopsida/Musci).

questions.question: Polytrichum is a:

options.B: Fern

questions.explanation: Polytrichum is a moss (Bryopsida), commonly known as hair cap moss.

questions.question: Sphagnum is economically important because:

options.B: It provides peat used as fuel and packing material, and absorbs pollutants
options.C: It is used to make medicines

questions.explanation: Sphagnum (peat moss) is economically important as: (1) it provides peat used as fuel in cold regions, (2) its antiseptic property makes it useful as packing material for trans-shipment of living material, (3) it forms peat in boggy areas.

questions.question: What gives Sphagnum its antiseptic property?

options.A: It contains high levels of phenols
options.B: It has large water-holding capacity and absorbs pathogenic fungi and bacteria

questions.explanation: Sphagnum has hygroscopic (large water-holding) property, large surface area and ability to absorb pathogenic fungi and bacteria, giving it antiseptic properties useful for packing living material.

questions.question: Sphagnum peat is used in cold regions as:

options.B: Fuel

questions.explanation: Dead and compressed Sphagnum plants form peat which is used as fuel in cold regions where other fuels are scarce.

questions.question: Sphagnum is used as packing material for trans-shipment of living material because:

options.B: It has capacity to hold water and has antiseptic property

questions.explanation: Sphagnum is used as packing material for trans-shipment of living material (plants, bulbs, etc.) because of its large water-holding capacity and antiseptic properties, keeping material moist and free from pathogens.

questions.question: The sex organs in mosses are produced at:

options.A: The base of the leafy gametophore
options.B: The apex of the leafy gametophore

questions.explanation: In mosses, the sex organs (antheridia and archegonia) are produced at the apex of the leafy gametophore.

questions.question: After fertilisation in mosses, the zygote develops into:

options.B: A sporophyte differentiated into foot, seta and capsule

questions.explanation: After fertilisation in mosses, the zygote develops into a sporophyte differentiated into foot, seta and capsule.

questions.question: The sporophyte of mosses is more elaborate than that of liverworts because:

options.B: It has a more developed capsule with complex structure for spore dispersal

questions.explanation: The sporophyte of mosses is more elaborate than that of liverworts. The capsule in mosses is more complex with a peristome (teeth-like structures) that regulate spore dispersal.

questions.question: Spores from moss capsule germinate to produce:

options.B: A seed

questions.explanation: Spores from the moss capsule germinate to produce a protonema — the first stage of the gametophyte, which is a creeping, green, branched, filamentous structure.

questions.question: What is the key difference between liverwort and moss gametophytes?

options.A: Liverworts have protonema; mosses do not
options.B: Mosses have protonema + leafy gametophore (2 stages); liverworts have only thalloid gametophyte
options.D: Mosses have thalloid gametophyte; liverworts have leafy gametophyte

questions.explanation: Key difference: Moss gametophyte has 2 stages (protonema + leafy gametophore). Liverwort gametophyte is primarily thalloid (prostrate, lobed thallus).

questions.question: Bryophytes play an important ecological role in which habitat?

options.B: In moist and humid areas, rocky terrain and as pioneer plants in ecological succession

questions.explanation: Bryophytes play important ecological roles in moist and humid areas, on rocky terrain (as pioneer plants in ecological succession), in preventing soil erosion, and in water retention.

questions.question: Bryophytes along with lichens are important as:

options.B: Pioneer organisms in ecological succession on bare rocks

questions.explanation: Bryophytes along with lichens are pioneer organisms in ecological succession — they colonize bare rocks first, breaking them down to form soil for other plants to grow.

questions.question: Which economic use is associated with Sphagnum in horticulture?

options.B: As compost material
options.C: As a soil additive for better water retention and as packing for plants

questions.explanation: Sphagnum is used in horticulture as a soil conditioner to improve water retention and as packing material for plants during transport.

questions.question: Some mosses provide food for:

options.B: Reindeer and other herbivores in cold regions (Sphagnum, Polytrichum)

questions.explanation: Mosses like Sphagnum and Polytrichum provide food for herbivores like reindeer and other animals in cold regions (tundra and taiga).

questions.question: The capsule of the bryophyte sporophyte produces spores by:

options.B: Meiosis, producing haploid spores

questions.explanation: The capsule (sporangium) of the bryophyte sporophyte produces haploid spores by meiosis.

questions.question: The ploidy of the bryophyte gametophyte is:

options.B: Triploid (3n)

questions.explanation: The bryophyte gametophyte is haploid (n). It produces gametes (antherozoids and eggs) by mitosis.

questions.question: The ploidy of the bryophyte sporophyte is:

options.B: Diploid (2n)

questions.explanation: The bryophyte sporophyte is diploid (2n). It develops from the zygote (formed by fertilisation) and produces haploid spores by meiosis.

questions.question: The gametes produced by the bryophyte gametophyte are formed by:

options.B: Mitosis

questions.explanation: The gametes (antherozoids and eggs) in bryophytes are produced by mitosis from the haploid gametophyte.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about bryophyte reproduction is CORRECT?

options.B: The gametophyte produces gametes by mitosis; the sporophyte produces spores by meiosis
options.D: Both gametophyte and sporophyte produce spores

questions.explanation: In bryophytes: the haploid gametophyte produces gametes by mitosis. The diploid sporophyte produces haploid spores by meiosis.

questions.question: Why is the bryophyte sporophyte described as partially or wholly dependent on the gametophyte?

options.B: Because it grows embedded in the gametophyte and gets nutrition from it
options.C: Because it is smaller than the gametophyte

questions.explanation: The bryophyte sporophyte is dependent on the gametophyte because the foot is embedded in the gametophyte tissue, absorbing water and nutrients from it.

questions.question: In the life cycle of bryophytes, meiosis occurs in the:

options.B: Antheridia and archegonia

questions.explanation: In bryophytes, meiosis occurs in the capsule (sporangium) of the sporophyte to produce haploid spores.

questions.question: In the life cycle of bryophytes, fertilisation occurs between:

options.B: Biflagellate antherozoid and egg cell

questions.explanation: In bryophytes, fertilisation occurs between the biflagellate antherozoid (male gamete from antheridium) and the egg cell (from archegonium) to form a diploid zygote.

questions.question: What is the ecological importance of bryophytes in preventing soil erosion?

options.A: They have no role in preventing soil erosion
options.B: Their dense mat-like growth covers and holds the soil, preventing erosion by wind and water
options.D: They produce chemicals that bind soil

questions.explanation: Bryophytes form dense mat-like growth on soil surfaces, which helps hold the soil particles together and prevent erosion by wind and water.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the structure of liverwort sporophyte?

options.A: It is large and photosynthetically independent
options.B: It is small and differentiated into foot, seta and capsule; dependent on gametophyte

questions.explanation: The liverwort sporophyte is small and differentiated into foot (embedded in gametophyte), seta (short stalk) and capsule (spore-producing structure). It is dependent on the gametophyte.

questions.question: The seta in liverwort sporophyte is typically described as:

options.B: Short or absent in many liverworts
options.D: The largest part of the sporophyte

questions.explanation: In many liverworts, the seta is short or relatively reduced. The capsule sits on a short seta.

questions.question: The archegonium is described as:

options.A: A round structure producing many eggs
options.B: A flask-shaped female sex organ containing a single egg
options.C: A branched structure producing male gametes

questions.explanation: The archegonium is a flask-shaped female sex organ found in bryophytes, pteridophytes and gymnosperms. It contains a single egg cell in its venter (base).

questions.question: The antheridium produces:

options.B: Spores

questions.explanation: The antheridium (male sex organ) in bryophytes produces biflagellate antherozoids (male gametes).

questions.question: Both antheridia and archegonia in bryophytes are described as 'jacketed'. This means:

options.A: They are covered by a hard shell
options.B: They are surrounded by a layer of sterile (non-reproductive) cells
options.D: They are covered by a leaf-like structure

questions.explanation: Both antheridia and archegonia in bryophytes are jacketed — they are surrounded by a layer of sterile (non-reproductive) cells that protect the gametes.

questions.question: Why are bryophytes important in carbon sequestration and climate regulation?

options.B: Sphagnum peat bogs store enormous amounts of carbon sequestered from atmosphere
options.C: They absorb CO₂ faster than any other plant
options.D: They have no role in carbon sequestration

questions.explanation: Sphagnum peat bogs are important carbon sinks — dead and partially decomposed Sphagnum accumulates as peat over thousands of years, storing large amounts of carbon from the atmosphere.

questions.question: In bryophytes, what initiates the alternation of generations cycle?

options.B: Germination of haploid spores to form gametophyte

questions.explanation: The alternation of generations cycle in bryophytes begins with germination of haploid spores (produced by meiosis in capsule) to form the free-living haploid gametophyte.

questions.question: What is the correct ploidy sequence in bryophyte life cycle?

options.A: Spore(n) → Gametophyte(n) → Gametes(n) → Zygote(2n) → Sporophyte(2n) → Spore(n)
options.B: Spore(2n) → Gametophyte(2n) → Gametes(n) → Zygote(n) → Sporophyte(n)

questions.explanation: Correct ploidy: Haploid spore(n) → haploid gametophyte(n) → gametes(n, by mitosis) → zygote(2n, after fertilisation) → diploid sporophyte(2n) → meiosis in capsule → haploid spore(n).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the DEPENDENT generation in bryophytes?

options.B: Sporophyte is dependent on gametophyte

questions.explanation: In bryophytes, the sporophyte is dependent on (partially or wholly) the gametophyte for nutrition and water. The gametophyte is the free-living, dominant generation.

questions.question: Which process during bryophyte reproduction requires a film of water?

options.B: Spore germination
options.C: Antherozoid swimming from antheridium to archegonium

questions.explanation: The biflagellate antherozoids require a film of water to swim from the antheridium to the archegonium. This is why bryophytes need water for sexual reproduction.

questions.question: Vegetative reproduction in mosses occurs by:

options.B: Fragmentation of protonema and secondary protonema

questions.explanation: Vegetative reproduction in mosses occurs by fragmentation of the protonema, formation of secondary protonema from fragments, and from underground stem-like structures (rhizomes).

questions.question: In which of the following does the protonema stage occur?

options.B: Mosses only

questions.explanation: The protonema stage is characteristic of mosses. Liverworts also have a brief protonema-like stage, but it is most prominent and characteristic in mosses.

questions.question: The leafy gametophore of moss is:

options.A: The first stage of the gametophyte
options.B: The second stage of the gametophyte — an upright structure with stem-like axis and leaf-like appendages

questions.explanation: The leafy gametophore is the second stage of the moss gametophyte — an upright, slender structure bearing stem-like axis with leaf-like appendages.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the difference between rhizoids of liverworts and mosses?

options.B: Both have multicellular branched rhizoids
options.C: Liverworts: unicellular rhizoids; Mosses: multicellular branched rhizoids

questions.explanation: Liverworts have unicellular rhizoids, while mosses have multicellular, branched rhizoids. This is one distinction between the two groups.

questions.question: In which group of bryophytes does vegetative reproduction by gemmae in gemma cups occur?

options.B: Liverworts (like Marchantia)

questions.explanation: Gemmae in gemma cups are characteristic vegetative reproductive structures of liverworts (like Marchantia). Mosses primarily reproduce vegetatively by fragmentation.

questions.question: What is the significance of bryophytes as pioneer organisms?

options.A: They directly produce soil from minerals
options.B: They colonize bare rocks along with lichens, and their remains contribute to soil formation, enabling establishment of other plants
options.C: They compete with other plants for resources
options.D: They only grow after other plants establish

questions.explanation: Bryophytes (along with lichens) are pioneer organisms that colonize bare rocks first. Their remains contribute to soil formation, enabling the establishment of other plants in ecological succession.

questions.question: The moss Funaria is commonly found in which environment?

options.B: Moist, shady places — on wet soil, rocks and walls near water

questions.explanation: Funaria (common cord moss) is found in moist, shady places — on wet soil, rocks, walls near water, and in forests in humid areas.

questions.question: What happens to the sporophyte after spores are dispersed in bryophytes?

options.B: It typically degenerates after spore dispersal

questions.explanation: After spore dispersal, the bryophyte sporophyte typically degenerates — it has completed its function of spore production.

questions.question: Which character of bryophytes is most similar to algae?

options.B: Production of seeds

questions.explanation: Like algae, bryophytes require water for fertilisation because their male gametes (antherozoids) are flagellated and motile, needing water to swim to the egg. This is the primary character linking them to algae.

questions.question: Which character of bryophytes is more advanced than algae?

options.B: Multicellular jacketed sex organs and embryo development on the mother plant

questions.explanation: Bryophytes are more advanced than algae in having: (1) multicellular jacketed sex organs (not present in most algae), (2) embryo development on the mother plant (the zygote develops into sporophyte while attached to gametophyte).

questions.question: The bryophyte body plan is described as 'thalloid' or 'leafy'. What is the difference?

options.B: Thalloid: flat, undifferentiated body (liverworts); Leafy: upright with stem-like axis and leaf-like structures (mosses)
options.C: Thalloid: has vascular tissue; Leafy: no vascular tissue
options.D: Thalloid: produces seeds; Leafy: produces spores

questions.explanation: Thalloid bryophytes (liverworts) have a flat, undifferentiated body appressed to substrate. Leafy bryophytes (mosses) have an upright stem-like axis with leaf-like appendages.

questions.question: The term 'Bryophyta' is derived from which language?

options.B: Greek — 'bryon' (moss) and 'phyton' (plant)

questions.explanation: Bryophyta is derived from Greek: 'bryon' meaning moss and 'phyton' meaning plant.

questions.question: In the bryophyte alternation of generations, the two types of nuclei division are:

options.B: Both mitosis
options.C: Mitosis in gametophyte (for gamete production) and meiosis in sporophyte capsule (for spore production)
options.D: Meiosis in gametophyte and mitosis in sporophyte

questions.explanation: In bryophytes: the haploid gametophyte produces gametes by mitosis. The diploid sporophyte capsule produces haploid spores by meiosis. Fertilization (gamete fusion) connects the two phases.

questions.question: Which of the following is an INCORRECT statement about bryophytes?

options.A: They are the amphibians of the plant kingdom
options.B: They have vascular tissue like pteridophytes
options.C: They are found in moist, shaded areas
options.D: The sporophyte is dependent on the gametophyte

questions.explanation: Bryophytes do NOT have vascular tissue (xylem and phloem). The absence of vascular tissue is a key feature distinguishing them from pteridophytes.

questions.question: Which of the following is the CORRECT sequence showing increasing complexity in plant groups?

options.B: Algae → Bryophytes → Pteridophytes → Gymnosperms → Angiosperms

questions.explanation: The correct sequence of increasing complexity: Algae (simplest) → Bryophytes → Pteridophytes → Gymnosperms → Angiosperms (most complex).

questions.question: How do bryophytes differ from pteridophytes?

options.A: Bryophytes have vascular tissue; pteridophytes do not
options.B: Bryophytes lack vascular tissue and have gametophyte-dominant life cycle; pteridophytes have vascular tissue and sporophyte-dominant life cycle
options.C: Bryophytes produce seeds; pteridophytes do not

questions.explanation: Key differences: Bryophytes lack vascular tissue, gametophyte is dominant. Pteridophytes have well-developed vascular tissue, sporophyte is dominant and independent.

questions.question: Why is the bryophyte considered an evolutionary link between algae and pteridophytes?

options.B: They share characters with both: need water for fertilisation (like algae) but have land adaptation and jacketed sex organs (unlike algae)
options.C: They have vascular tissue like pteridophytes
options.D: They are completely aquatic like algae

questions.explanation: Bryophytes are evolutionary links between algae and pteridophytes: they need water for fertilisation (like algae) but are primarily terrestrial with multicellular jacketed sex organs, embryo development on mother plant (features leading toward pteridophytes).

questions.question: Which bryophytes are found in wetlands and peat bogs?

options.B: Marchantia

questions.explanation: Sphagnum is found in wetlands and peat bogs. Its dead remains accumulate as peat, forming peat bogs in cold regions.

questions.question: What is a peat bog and why is it ecologically significant?

options.A: A type of forest dominated by pine trees
options.B: A wetland habitat dominated by Sphagnum that stores large amounts of carbon and provides unique biodiversity

questions.explanation: A peat bog is a wetland habitat dominated by Sphagnum moss. Dead Sphagnum accumulates as peat, storing huge amounts of carbon (carbon sink). Peat bogs are ecologically significant for carbon sequestration, water storage, and unique biodiversity.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a member of Bryophyta?

options.B: Marchantia

questions.explanation: Selaginella is a pteridophyte (vascular plant), not a bryophyte. Funaria, Marchantia and Sphagnum are bryophytes.

questions.question: What is the function of the foot in the bryophyte sporophyte?

options.B: Absorbing water and nutrients from the gametophyte tissue

questions.explanation: The foot in the bryophyte sporophyte is the embedded portion that absorbs water and nutrients from the surrounding gametophyte tissue, as the sporophyte is dependent on the gametophyte.

questions.question: In mosses, the sporophyte is:

options.B: Partially dependent on gametophyte — foot embedded in gametophyte, capsule may photosynthesize
options.C: Completely dependent, with no photosynthetic ability

questions.explanation: The moss sporophyte is partially dependent on the gametophyte — the foot is embedded in gametophyte tissue. However, the capsule (and sometimes the seta) contains chlorophyll and can photosynthesize partially.

questions.question: Which structure in mosses is green and photosynthetic in the sporophyte?

options.B: The seta and capsule

questions.explanation: In mosses, the seta and capsule may contain chlorophyll and can photosynthesize partially. However, the sporophyte still depends on the gametophyte.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes alternation of generations?

options.A: Alternation between asexual and sexual generations
options.B: Alternation between haploid gametophyte and diploid sporophyte generations
options.D: Alternation between vegetative and reproductive phases

questions.explanation: Alternation of generations refers to the alternation between haploid gametophyte (producing gametes) and diploid sporophyte (producing spores) phases in the life cycle.

questions.question: In bryophytes, does the sporophyte generation ever become free-living and dominant?

options.B: Yes, in mosses only
options.C: No, sporophyte is always dependent on gametophyte in bryophytes

questions.explanation: In bryophytes, the sporophyte never becomes free-living and dominant — it is always at least partially dependent on the gametophyte. This is in contrast to pteridophytes where the sporophyte is dominant and free-living.

questions.question: Protonema is:

options.B: The first, filamentous stage of the moss gametophyte developed from spore germination

questions.explanation: Protonema is the first stage of the moss gametophyte — a creeping, green, branched, filamentous structure that develops from the germination of a haploid spore.

questions.question: What is the key feature that distinguishes bryophytes from all algae?

options.A: Bryophytes are autotrophic; algae are heterotrophic
options.B: Bryophytes have multicellular jacketed sex organs and embryo development on parent plant; algae have unicellular or non-jacketed sex organs
options.C: Bryophytes are always terrestrial; algae are always aquatic
options.D: Bryophytes have chlorophyll; algae do not

questions.explanation: The key features distinguishing bryophytes from algae are: (1) multicellular jacketed sex organs (antheridia and archegonia), (2) embryo development on the parent (gametophyte) plant — the embryophyte condition.

questions.question: The sporophyte produces spores by meiosis. These spores are:

options.B: Haploid

questions.explanation: The sporophyte (2n) produces haploid (n) spores by meiosis. These spores germinate to produce the haploid gametophyte.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of the entire plant body of a liverwort?

options.B: A small, flat, lobed, ribbon-like thallus lying on moist substrate
options.C: An upright plant with well-developed leaves

questions.explanation: A liverwort is a small, flat, lobed, ribbon-like thallus (like Marchantia) lying closely on the moist substrate. This describes its thalloid plant body.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly pairs a bryophyte with its class?

options.B: Marchantia - Bryopsida

questions.explanation: Sphagnum belongs to Bryopsida (mosses). Funaria also belongs to Bryopsida. Marchantia and Riccia belong to Hepaticopsida (liverworts).

questions.question: Which two groups are included in bryophytes as mentioned in NCERT Chapter 3?

options.B: Liverworts and mosses

questions.explanation: In NCERT Chapter 3, bryophytes are divided into liverworts and mosses (the two groups discussed in detail). Hornworts are also mentioned but not discussed in detail.

questions.question: Which economic product obtained from Sphagnum is used in cold regions as fuel?

options.B: Petroleum

questions.explanation: Peat, formed from dead and compressed Sphagnum plants, is used as fuel in cold regions like Ireland, Scotland, and parts of Russia where other fuels are scarce.

questions.question: The ecological succession process in which bryophytes are involved as pioneer organisms primarily occurs on:

options.B: Bare rocks — bryophytes contribute to soil formation for succession

questions.explanation: Bryophytes act as pioneer organisms in ecological succession on bare rocks. They break down rocks, trap organic matter, and their remains contribute to soil formation, enabling succession of other plant communities.

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about the life span of the bryophyte sporophyte?

options.A: It is perennial and lives for many years
options.B: It is relatively short-lived, functioning only to produce and disperse spores
options.D: It produces new sporophytes by vegetative reproduction

questions.explanation: The bryophyte sporophyte is relatively short-lived — it exists primarily to produce spores by meiosis and disperse them. After spore dispersal, it typically degenerates.

questions.question: Gemmae (gemma cups) are MOST characteristic of which bryophytes?

options.B: Liverworts (especially Marchantia)

questions.explanation: Gemmae in gemma cups are most characteristic of liverworts, especially Marchantia. They are a vegetative reproductive structure unique to this group.

questions.question: Which bryophyte group is named for the liver-like shape of the plant body?

options.B: Liverworts

questions.explanation: Liverworts are named for the liver-like lobed shape of the plant body (thallus). The class name Hepaticopsida comes from 'hepat' meaning liver.

questions.question: The protonema stage in the moss life cycle is characterized by:

options.B: Being haploid, filamentous, green, branched — develops from spore germination

questions.explanation: The protonema is the first haploid stage of the moss gametophyte. It is a creeping, green, branched, filamentous structure that develops from germination of a haploid spore.

questions.question: What is the significance of multicellular jacketed sex organs in bryophytes?

options.A: They increase the size of gametes
options.B: They protect the gametes from desiccation and mechanical injury

questions.explanation: Multicellular jacketed sex organs in bryophytes protect the gametes from desiccation (drying out) and mechanical injury — an adaptation to the terrestrial environment.

questions.question: Compared to algae, what is the additional feature in bryophytes regarding embryo development?

options.B: The embryo (young sporophyte/zygote) remains attached to and develops within the gametophyte (mother plant)
options.D: Embryo is enclosed in a seed

questions.explanation: In bryophytes, the embryo (zygote developing into young sporophyte) remains attached to the gametophyte (mother plant) and develops on it — this is the embryophyte condition, not seen in algae.

questions.question: Asexual reproduction in liverworts by gemmae is advantageous because:

options.B: It allows rapid multiplication of genetically identical individuals without water requirement

questions.explanation: Gemmae allow rapid vegetative multiplication producing genetically identical individuals without requiring water (unlike sexual reproduction which needs water for antherozoid swimming).

questions.question: Which of the following shows vegetative reproduction in liverworts?

options.B: Production of antheridia and archegonia

questions.explanation: Vegetative reproduction in liverworts occurs by fragmentation of thalli and through formation of gemmae in gemma cups.

questions.question: In bryophytes, the archegonium produces:

options.B: A single egg cell

questions.explanation: The archegonium in bryophytes is a flask-shaped female sex organ that produces a single egg cell in its venter (base).

questions.question: What would happen if there were no water during the bryophyte reproductive season?

options.A: Sexual reproduction would not be affected
options.B: Sexual reproduction would fail as antherozoids cannot swim to archegonium

questions.explanation: If there were no water, sexual reproduction in bryophytes would fail because the biflagellate antherozoids (male gametes) need water as a medium to swim to the archegonium for fertilisation.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most economically significant moss?

options.B: Polytrichum

questions.explanation: Sphagnum (peat moss) is the most economically significant moss — used as fuel, packing material, soil conditioner, and is ecologically important as a carbon sink.

questions.question: The capsule of the bryophyte sporophyte is equivalent to which structure in algae?

options.B: Archegonium

questions.explanation: The capsule (sporangium) of the bryophyte sporophyte is equivalent to the zoosporangium of algae — both are structures that produce spores by meiosis (in bryophytes) or mitosis (zoospores in algae).

questions.question: The green, photosynthetic, free-living generation in bryophytes is the:

options.B: Gametophyte

questions.explanation: The green, photosynthetic, free-living generation in bryophytes is the gametophyte (haploid) — this is the dominant phase.

questions.question: Compared to the sporophyte, the gametophyte of bryophytes is:

options.B: More prominent, free-living and dominant

questions.explanation: In bryophytes, the gametophyte is more prominent, free-living, and dominant compared to the sporophyte which is smaller and dependent on the gametophyte.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct description of the bryophyte plant body?

options.A: Attached to substrate by true roots, possessing xylem and phloem
options.B: Attached to substrate by rhizoids, lacking true vascular tissue, more differentiated than algae

questions.explanation: The bryophyte plant body is: attached to substrate by rhizoids (not true roots), lacks true vascular tissue (xylem/phloem), and is more differentiated than algae with leaf-like, stem-like structures (in mosses).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies where the bryophyte sporophyte develops?

options.B: On or within the gametophyte, remaining attached

questions.explanation: The bryophyte sporophyte develops on or within the gametophyte, remaining attached to it. The foot is embedded in the gametophyte tissue.

questions.question: Bryophytes are found in high humidity areas. What happens to them in dry conditions?

options.B: They can become dormant and survive dehydration, reviving when moisture returns

questions.explanation: Many bryophytes are poikilohydric — they can lose water and become dormant (dried up), then revive (revival of dried specimens) when moisture becomes available again.

questions.question: What is the difference between a 'thalloid' liverwort and a 'leafy' liverwort?

options.A: Thalloid: no differentiation at all; Leafy: has leaf-like appendages but still no true stem
options.B: Thalloid: has vascular tissue; Leafy: no vascular tissue
options.C: Thalloid: produces seeds; Leafy: produces spores

questions.explanation: Thalloid liverworts (e.g., Marchantia) have a flat, undifferentiated thalloid body. Leafy liverworts (e.g., Porella) have leaf-like appendages arranged on a stem-like axis, but still lack true vascular tissue.

questions.question: Porella is a leafy liverwort. What does this mean?

options.B: It has leaf-like appendages on a stem-like axis but is still classified as a liverwort (no true vascular tissue)
options.C: It has true leaves with vascular bundles

questions.explanation: Porella is a leafy liverwort — it has leaf-like appendages on a stem-like axis, making it look like a tiny leafy plant, but it is still classified as a liverwort and lacks true vascular tissue.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Marchantia is CORRECT?

options.B: It is a liverwort (Hepaticopsida) with thalloid gametophyte, gemma cups for vegetative reproduction and separate male/female reproductive plants

questions.explanation: Marchantia is a liverwort (Hepaticopsida) with a thalloid gametophyte that reproduces vegetatively by gemmae in gemma cups and sexually by antheridia and archegonia on separate male and female plants (dioecious).

questions.question: The antherozoids of bryophytes swim to the archegonium attracted by:

options.B: Chemical substances (chemotropism) secreted by the archegonium

questions.explanation: The biflagellate antherozoids are attracted to the archegonium by chemical substances (chemotaxis) secreted by the archegonium, guiding them to the egg cell.

questions.question: Which characteristic feature proves that bryophytes are 'embryophytes' (land plants)?

options.B: Their embryo (young sporophyte) is retained and develops on the mother gametophyte

questions.explanation: Bryophytes are embryophytes because their embryo (young sporophyte developing from zygote) is retained on and dependent on the mother gametophyte plant. This embryo retention is a key land plant characteristic.

questions.question: The leafy stage of the moss gametophyte is called:

options.B: Gametophore

questions.explanation: The second stage of the moss gametophyte — the upright, slender, leafy structure bearing stem-like axis with leaf-like appendages — is called the gametophore.

questions.question: Buds from the protonema give rise to:

options.B: Leafy gametophores

questions.explanation: Numerous buds are formed on the protonema. These buds give rise to the leafy gametophore — the second stage of the moss gametophyte.

questions.question: Why are bryophytes NOT considered as fully terrestrial plants?

options.B: Because they are completely dependent on water for sexual reproduction (antherozoids need water to swim)

questions.explanation: Bryophytes are not fully terrestrial because they are still dependent on water for sexual reproduction — the biflagellate antherozoids need water to swim to the archegonium. This is why they are called 'amphibians of the plant kingdom'.

questions.question: What is the significance of the 'rhizoid' in bryophytes?

options.A: It conducts water and minerals like xylem
options.B: It anchors the plant to the substrate and absorbs water

questions.explanation: Rhizoids in bryophytes anchor the plant to the substrate and absorb water. However, they are not true roots and lack vascular tissue.

questions.question: Which of the following is correctly described as the 'first land plants'?

options.B: Bryophytes (earliest known land plants)

questions.explanation: Bryophytes are considered among the earliest land plants. They represent the transition from aquatic (algae) to terrestrial life.

questions.question: In the bryophyte life cycle, the gametes fuse to form a:

options.B: Diploid zygote (2n)

questions.explanation: In bryophytes, the fusion of haploid (n) male antherozoid with haploid (n) egg produces a diploid (2n) zygote.

questions.question: The zygote in bryophytes is the first cell of which generation?

options.B: Sporophyte

questions.explanation: The zygote (2n) in bryophytes is the first cell of the sporophyte generation. It develops into the sporophyte (foot, seta, capsule).

questions.question: The spore of bryophytes is the first cell of which generation?

options.B: Gametophyte

questions.explanation: The haploid spore of bryophytes is the first cell of the gametophyte generation. It germinates to produce the protonema (in mosses) or directly the thalloid gametophyte (in liverworts).

questions.question: Which correctly describes the evolutionary significance of bryophytes?

options.A: They represent a dead end in plant evolution
options.B: They show the transition from aquatic algae to terrestrial vascular plants, serving as an evolutionary link

questions.explanation: Bryophytes show the transition from aquatic (algae) to terrestrial life. They have terrestrial adaptations (land habitat, rhizoids, jacketed sex organs) but retain aquatic characters (need water for fertilisation), making them evolutionary links.

questions.question: Which of the following is found in bryophytes but NOT in algae?

options.B: Multicellular jacketed sex organs (antheridia and archegonia)

questions.explanation: Multicellular jacketed sex organs (antheridia and archegonia) are found in bryophytes but not in algae (algae have unicellular or non-jacketed sex organs). This is a key advancement of bryophytes over algae.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly compares liverworts and mosses?

options.A: Liverworts: unicellular rhizoids, thalloid body, gemmae cups; Mosses: multicellular branched rhizoids, leafy gametophore with protonema stage
options.B: Liverworts: multicellular rhizoids; Mosses: unicellular rhizoids
options.D: Mosses: thalloid gametophyte; Liverworts: leafy gametophyte with protonema

questions.explanation: Key comparison: Liverworts have unicellular rhizoids, thalloid body, and gemmae in gemma cups. Mosses have multicellular branched rhizoids, leafy gametophore, and a protonema stage.

questions.question: Sphagnum has large water-holding capacity because:

options.A: It has true roots that absorb water
options.B: It has large dead hyaline cells that store water

questions.explanation: Sphagnum has large dead hyaline (transparent, empty) cells interspersed with living cells. These dead hyaline cells absorb and store large amounts of water, giving Sphagnum its remarkable water-holding capacity.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the sequence of development of the moss gametophyte?

options.B: Spore → Protonema → Buds → Leafy gametophore

questions.explanation: Development of moss gametophyte: Haploid spore → germinates → Protonema (creeping, filamentous) → forms buds → Leafy gametophore (upright, with stem-like axis and leaf-like appendages).

questions.question: The sex organs in bryophytes are 'multicellular' — what is the significance of this?

options.A: It makes them easier to disperse
options.B: It provides protection and nutrition to developing gametes (unlike unicellular sex organs in algae)
options.C: It increases the number of gametes produced

questions.explanation: Multicellular sex organs in bryophytes are significant because they provide protection (the jacket of sterile cells) and nutrition to developing gametes, which is an advancement over unicellular sex organs in algae.

questions.question: In which of the following does a 'protonema' stage NOT occur?

options.B: Polytrichum

questions.explanation: Marchantia is a liverwort — it does not have a prominent protonema stage. The protonema is characteristic of mosses (Funaria, Polytrichum, Sphagnum).

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct statement about the bryophyte sporophyte?

options.B: It is diploid, dependent on gametophyte, differentiated into foot, seta and capsule

questions.explanation: The bryophyte sporophyte is diploid (2n), at least partially dependent on the haploid gametophyte, and differentiated into foot (anchor/absorption), seta (stalk) and capsule (spore production by meiosis).

questions.question: Which of the following is INCORRECT about the ecological role of bryophytes?

options.A: They act as pioneer plants in ecological succession
options.B: They prevent soil erosion
options.C: They fix atmospheric nitrogen like Rhizobium
options.D: Sphagnum peat bogs serve as carbon sinks

questions.explanation: Bryophytes do NOT fix atmospheric nitrogen like Rhizobium. Their ecological roles include: pioneer plant in succession, preventing soil erosion, and Sphagnum peat bogs as carbon sinks.

questions.question: The process by which the liverwort sporophyte produces spores is:

options.B: Meiosis inside the capsule

questions.explanation: The liverwort sporophyte produces haploid spores by meiosis inside the capsule.

questions.question: Marchantia is described as dioecious. What does this mean?

options.A: Both sex organs on same plant
options.B: Male and female reproductive structures on separate plants

questions.explanation: Marchantia is dioecious — male (antheridiophores with antheridia) and female (archegoniophores with archegonia) reproductive structures are borne on separate plants.

questions.question: The gemma cups on Marchantia contain:

options.B: Green, multicellular, asexual gemmae

questions.explanation: Gemma cups on Marchantia contain green, multicellular, asexual gemmae. When detached, these germinate to form new, genetically identical gametophytes.

questions.question: After fertilisation in Marchantia, where does the sporophyte develop?

options.B: Within the archegonium on the female thallus

questions.explanation: After fertilisation in Marchantia, the sporophyte develops within the archegonium on the female thallus (archegoniophore), remaining attached to and dependent on the gametophyte.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct match?

options.B: Funaria - Hepaticopsida

questions.explanation: Sphagnum belongs to Bryopsida (mosses). Marchantia belongs to Hepaticopsida (liverworts). Funaria and Polytrichum belong to Bryopsida.

questions.question: The fact that bryophytes need water for sexual reproduction limits their distribution to:

options.B: Moist and humid areas where water is available during reproductive season

questions.explanation: The dependence on water for sexual reproduction limits bryophyte distribution to moist and humid areas where water is available during the reproductive season.

questions.question: Which of the following is a FALSE statement about bryophytes?

options.B: The gametophyte is the dominant phase
options.D: They are found in moist habitats

questions.explanation: Bryophytes do NOT produce seeds — they reproduce by spores. Seed production is a feature of seed plants (gymnosperms and angiosperms).

questions.question: In the bryophyte life cycle, which event marks the beginning of the sporophyte generation?

options.B: Germination of spore

questions.explanation: Fertilisation (fusion of antherozoid and egg to form diploid zygote) marks the beginning of the sporophyte generation in bryophytes.

questions.question: In the bryophyte life cycle, which event marks the beginning of the gametophyte generation?

options.B: Germination of haploid spore

questions.explanation: Germination of the haploid spore marks the beginning of the gametophyte generation in bryophytes.

questions.question: The ecological term for Sphagnum's role in maintaining ecosystem water levels is:

options.B: Water table regulation — Sphagnum peat bogs hold enormous amounts of water

questions.explanation: Sphagnum peat bogs hold enormous amounts of water (up to 20 times their own weight), helping regulate local water tables and maintaining ecosystem water levels.

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about bryophyte sex organs?

options.A: Male organ: archegonium; Female organ: antheridium
options.B: Male organ: antheridium (produces antherozoids); Female organ: archegonium (produces egg)

questions.explanation: In bryophytes: Antheridium is the male sex organ (produces biflagellate antherozoids). Archegonium is the female sex organ (flask-shaped, produces a single egg).

questions.question: How does the bryophyte sporophyte compare to the algal thallus in terms of complexity?

options.A: Sporophyte is simpler than algal thallus
options.B: Both are equally simple
options.C: Sporophyte is differentiated into distinct parts (foot, seta, capsule) unlike most algal thalli
options.D: Sporophyte is identical to algal thallus

questions.explanation: The bryophyte sporophyte is differentiated into distinct parts (foot, seta, capsule) showing more organization than most algal thalli which are undifferentiated.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the economic importance of mosses?

options.A: They are used as food for humans
options.B: Sphagnum provides peat (fuel), packing material, and absorbs oil spills
options.C: They are used to make medicines for treating cancer
options.D: They are used as biofuel for automobiles

questions.explanation: Mosses are economically important in several ways: Sphagnum provides peat used as fuel; its water-holding capacity makes it useful as packing material; it has been used to absorb oil spills due to its large water/liquid holding capacity.

questions.question: Which is NOT found in bryophytes?

options.B: Antheridia

questions.explanation: Bryophytes lack vascular tissue (xylem and phloem). They have rhizoids, antheridia and archegonia, but are non-vascular plants.

questions.question: The correct relationship between moss protonema and gametophore is:

options.B: Protonema is first gametophyte stage; gametophore is second gametophyte stage
options.C: Protonema is sporophyte; gametophore is gametophyte

questions.explanation: Both protonema and gametophore are stages of the same haploid gametophyte generation. Protonema (filamentous) is the first stage; the leafy gametophore is the second, more complex stage.

questions.question: In Marchantia, where are antheridia found?

options.A: On the underside of the thallus
options.B: On special structures called antheridiophores on the male thallus

questions.explanation: In Marchantia, antheridia are borne on special umbrella-like structures called antheridiophores on the male plant.

questions.question: In Marchantia, where are archegonia found?

options.A: On special structures called archegoniophores on the female thallus
options.B: On the gemma cups

questions.explanation: In Marchantia, archegonia are borne on special umbrella-like structures called archegoniophores on the female plant.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct statement about vegetative reproduction in mosses?

options.A: Mosses reproduce vegetatively by gemmae in gemma cups
options.B: Mosses reproduce vegetatively by fragmentation and secondary protonema formation
options.D: Mosses reproduce vegetatively by binary fission

questions.explanation: Mosses reproduce vegetatively by fragmentation — fragments of protonema or leafy gametophore can give rise to secondary protonema, which then forms new gametophores.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs is correctly matched?

options.B: Mosses - protonema - multicellular branched rhizoids
options.C: Liverworts - no protonema - multicellular branched rhizoids

questions.explanation: Mosses: have a protonema stage AND multicellular branched rhizoids. Liverworts: do not have a prominent protonema stage AND have unicellular rhizoids.

questions.question: Which is the CORRECT summary of economic importance of bryophytes?

options.B: Sphagnum as fuel, packing material and soil conditioner; ecological role as pioneer plants and carbon sinks; food for herbivores
options.C: Only used as food for humans

questions.explanation: Bryophyte economic importance: (1) Sphagnum peat as fuel, (2) Sphagnum as packing material (water holding, antiseptic), (3) soil conditioner, (4) ecological pioneer plants, (5) carbon sinks (peat bogs), (6) food for reindeer and other herbivores.

questions.question: In an examination, a student says 'bryophytes are photosynthetic amphibians that can live on land but need water for reproduction.' Is this statement correct?

options.B: Mostly correct — bryophytes are photosynthetic plants (not animals) that live on land but need water for sexual reproduction
options.D: Incorrect because bryophytes don't need water for reproduction

questions.explanation: The statement is mostly correct — bryophytes ARE photosynthetic (not heterotrophic), they DO live on land, and they DO need water for sexual reproduction. The only issue is calling them 'amphibians' (they are plants, not animals, but are metaphorically called 'amphibians of the plant kingdom').

questions.question: Which character shows that bryophytes are more adapted to land than algae?

options.B: Have multicellular jacketed sex organs protecting gametes from desiccation, and rhizoids for anchorage

questions.explanation: Bryophytes show better land adaptation than algae through: (1) multicellular jacketed sex organs (protect gametes from desiccation), (2) rhizoids (anchorage to substrate), (3) protection of developing embryo within the mother plant.

questions.question: The capsule (sporangium) in bryophytes produces spores. These spores are:

options.B: Haploid and produce protonema (mosses) or thalloid gametophyte (liverworts) on germination

questions.explanation: Capsule produces haploid spores by meiosis. On germination: in mosses, spores produce protonema → gametophore; in liverworts, spores produce thalloid gametophyte.

questions.question: Why can't bryophytes grow as tall as vascular plants (pteridophytes, gymnosperms, angiosperms)?

options.B: They lack vascular tissue (xylem and phloem) to efficiently transport water and nutrients to great heights

questions.explanation: Bryophytes cannot grow tall because they lack vascular tissue (xylem and phloem) which in higher plants efficiently transports water and nutrients to great heights. Water in bryophytes moves slowly by capillarity and osmosis.

questions.question: What does 'haploid generation' mean in the context of bryophyte alternation of generations?

options.B: The generation with n chromosomes — the gametophyte that produces gametes

questions.explanation: Haploid generation means the generation with n (haploid) chromosomes — in bryophytes this is the gametophyte, which produces gametes by mitosis.

questions.question: A student observes a plant in a moist, shaded area. It has a flat, lobed, ribbon-like thallus closely appressed to wet rock, with small cup-shaped structures on top. This plant is most likely:

options.B: A liverwort (like Marchantia)

questions.explanation: The description — flat, lobed, ribbon-like thallus on wet rock with small cup-shaped structures (gemma cups) — perfectly describes a liverwort like Marchantia.

questions.question: A student observes a plant with an upright, tiny structure bearing miniature leaf-like structures arranged around a central axis, attached to the soil by multicellular branched structures, and a long capsule on a stalk at the top. This is most likely a:

options.B: Fern

questions.explanation: The description — upright structure with leaf-like appendages, multicellular branched rhizoids, capsule on seta — describes a moss like Funaria.

questions.question: Which group of plants was most dominant on land before the evolution of vascular plants?

options.B: Angiosperms

questions.explanation: Bryophytes were among the first and most dominant land plants before the evolution of vascular plants (pteridophytes). They colonized terrestrial habitats before vascular plants evolved.

questions.question: Which of the following is a 'warp and weft' pattern of alternation of generations described in bryophytes?

options.A: Alternating between sporophyte and gametophyte, with gametophyte dominant
options.B: No alternation — only one phase

questions.explanation: In bryophytes, there is a clear alternation between sporophyte (diploid, dependent) and gametophyte (haploid, dominant) generations. The gametophyte is dominant.

questions.question: Select the CORRECT set of characteristics of Bryophyta:

options.A: Vascular tissue present, gametophyte dominant, seeds produced
options.B: No vascular tissue, gametophyte dominant, water needed for fertilization, sporophyte dependent
options.C: Vascular tissue present, sporophyte dominant, seeds produced
options.D: No vascular tissue, sporophyte dominant, no water needed for fertilization

questions.explanation: Bryophyta characteristics: No vascular tissue, gametophyte is dominant phase, water is needed for fertilization (biflagellate antherozoids), sporophyte is dependent on gametophyte.

questions.question: What is the 'calyptra' in bryophytes?

options.B: A protective covering (derived from archegonium wall) over the developing capsule

questions.explanation: The calyptra is a protective cap or covering derived from the archegonium wall that covers the developing capsule of the sporophyte in many bryophytes.

questions.question: The sporophyte of liverworts is LEAST developed compared to mosses because:

options.A: Liverworts are more primitive, with a simpler sporophyte (short or absent seta, simple capsule)
options.B: Liverworts are more advanced
options.C: The liverwort sporophyte has more complex capsule

questions.explanation: Liverwort sporophyte is simpler (less developed) — the seta is often short or absent, and the capsule structure is simpler. Moss sporophyte is more elaborate with a longer seta and more complex capsule with peristome.

questions.question: Which group of bryophytes provides the highest ecological services globally?

options.B: Hornworts

questions.explanation: Sphagnum mosses (peat mosses) provide the highest global ecological services — storing enormous amounts of carbon in peat bogs, regulating water tables, providing unique biodiversity habitat.

questions.question: In the NCERT chapter, bryophytes are described in terms of which two main groups?

options.B: Liverworts and mosses

questions.explanation: In NCERT Chapter 3, bryophytes are discussed in terms of two main groups: liverworts and mosses.

questions.question: Mosses form dense mats on rocks and soil. What is the ecological significance of this?

options.A: They block sunlight for other plants
options.B: They bind soil, prevent erosion, retain moisture, and contribute to soil formation through accumulation of organic matter
options.C: They compete with other plants for nutrients

questions.explanation: Moss mats on rocks and soil: bind soil (prevent erosion), retain moisture, and through accumulation of dead organic matter contribute to soil formation, creating habitat for other organisms.

questions.question: What is the main difference between spores and seeds?

options.A: Spores are multicellular; seeds are unicellular
options.B: Spores are haploid, single-celled, no food reserve; seeds are diploid, multicellular, have food reserve
options.D: Seeds are produced by meiosis; spores are not

questions.explanation: Key differences: Spores (in bryophytes) are haploid, single-celled, have no stored food reserve. Seeds (in gymnosperms/angiosperms) are diploid, multicellular (contain embryo + food reserve/endosperm + seed coat).

questions.question: A student observes that a plant cannot grow without a moist environment and dies in dry conditions. Its reproductive success depends on rainfall. This is most likely a:

options.B: Angiosperm

questions.explanation: Bryophytes are most dependent on moist environments — they grow in moist habitats and require water (rainfall) for sexual reproduction. Their biflagellate antherozoids must swim to the archegonium.

questions.question: Which bryophyte group is considered most primitive?

options.B: Liverworts (Hepaticopsida) — generally considered more primitive

questions.explanation: Liverworts are generally considered more primitive among bryophytes — they have simpler thalloid bodies, simpler sporophytes (shorter seta, simpler capsule), and unicellular rhizoids. Mosses are more advanced with protonema stage, leafy gametophore, multicellular rhizoids, and more complex sporophyte.

questions.question: If a bryophyte's capsule is removed, what would happen to reproduction?

options.A: Only sexual reproduction would be affected
options.B: Asexual spore production would be eliminated, affecting colonization of new habitats
options.D: Only vegetative reproduction would be affected

questions.explanation: If the capsule is removed, no spores would be produced. This would eliminate asexual (spore) reproduction, significantly affecting the plant's ability to colonize new habitats, as spores are the main dispersal unit.

questions.question: The complete description of bryophytes includes which of the following features?

options.A: Vascular tissue, true roots, stem and leaves, produce seeds
options.B: Non-vascular, rhizoids, thalloid or leafy gametophyte dominant, sporophyte dependent, need water for fertilization
options.C: Vascular, no seeds, dominant sporophyte, independent gametophyte

questions.explanation: Complete description of bryophytes: Non-vascular (no xylem/phloem), anchored by rhizoids, thalloid (liverworts) or leafy (mosses) gametophyte is dominant and free-living, sporophyte is dependent on gametophyte, water needed for fertilisation (biflagellate antherozoids).
